plan.contextChunk:
edge constraints and we thus don't have enough local information to
infer the surface orientation. Therefore, we need some criteria in order
to propagate information from the boundaries, where we do have
information about the 3D structure, into the interior of flat image
regions. This problem is common in many visual domains.



:::{.column-margin}
An image patch without context is not enough to infer its 3D shape.

![](figures/simplesystem/cube_propagartion_1.png){width="44%"} 

The same patch shown in the original image (right):

![](figures/simplesystem/cube_propagartion_2.png){width="44%"}

Information about its 3D orientation its propagated from the surrounding edges.
:::


In this case we will assume that the object faces are planar. Thus, flat
image regions impose the following constraints on the local 3D
structure: $$\begin{aligned}
\partial^2 Y / \partial x^2 &= 0  \\
\partial^2 Y / \partial y^2 &= 0 \\  
\partial^2 Y / \partial y \partial x &= 0 
\end{aligned}$$ That is, the second order derivative of $Y$ should be
zero. As before, we want to approximate the continuous partial
derivatives. The approximation to the second derivative can be obtained
by applying twice the first order derivative approximated by equations

elements: green cube with clearly defined edges
dashed circle on the face of the cube
arrows pointing towards the center of the circle from four directions

interactions.teaches: Allows you to rotate the cube to view it from different angles, illustrating how 3D orientation is perceived.
Orbits around the cube to give a full view of its 3D structure, emphasizing the importance of edge information.

demo_steps.title: Initial cube view
demo_steps.narration: Here is the cube in its initial position. Notice how the dashed circle and arrows suggest that information from the edges is propagating inward.

demo_steps.title: Rotate to see edges
demo_steps.narration: Rotate the cube. As you change its orientation, consider how the visible edges provide cues about the cube's 3D shape.

demo_steps.title: Understand propagation
demo_steps.narration: Notice how the arrows indicate the flow of information from the edges. This demonstrates how edge constraints help infer 3D structure over flat regions.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Perspective view at an angle, showing two adjacent faces of the cube with the dashed circle and arrows visible.

elements: green cube with clearly defined edges and flat faces
dashed circle centered on one of the cube's faces
four arrows originating from the edges towards the center of the dashed circle
corner labels indicating 3D coordinates (e.g., A, B, C)
edge labels to identify propagation direction (e.g., X+, Y+)

interactions.teaches: This interaction allows you to rotate the cube, illustrating how observing the cube from various angles provides different insights into its 3D structure.
Hover over edge labels to see how information propagates along the X and Y axes, which is critical for understanding how edges affect perceived orientation.

demo_steps.title: Initial view with labels
demo_steps.narration: Observe the cube with labeled corners and edges. Notice the dashed circle on the face, with arrows indicating direction of information propagation from the edges.

demo_steps.title: Rotating the cube
demo_steps.narration: Rotate the cube by dragging. Pay attention to how different faces and edges become visible, enhancing your perception of the cube's 3D orientation.

demo_steps.title: Edge-directed propagation
demo_steps.narration: Notice how the arrows guide the eye from the edges to the center circle, representing the propagation of 3D surface knowledge inward.

demo_steps.title: Label interactions
demo_steps.narration: Hover over the edge labels to see brief explanations on how each edge promotes information flow into the interior, important for inferring 3D structure over flat regions.

plan.camera_suggestion: Isometric view showing three adjacent faces of the cube, prominently displaying the face with the dashed circle and arrows.
